tasks.task: Where does Django resolve an incoming URL path to a view (URL dispatch)?
tasks.without_answer: Django resolves an incoming URL path to a view in django/urls/resolvers.py â€” the recursive dispatcher is URLResolver.resolve(self, path) at line 670 (class URLResolver, line 503). It matches the path against its own pattern, then iterates self.url_patterns, recursively calling pattern.resolve(new_path) to walk the URLconf tree, returning a ResolverMatch. The leaf match that binds the path to the actual view callback is URLPattern.resolve(self, path) at line 471 (class URLPattern, line 422), which returns ResolverMatch(self.callback, ...). The public entry point is the module-level function resolve(path, urlconf=None) in django/urls/base.py line 22, which delegates to get_resolver(urlconf).resolve(path).
tasks.withdb_answer: django/urls/resolvers.py â€” URL dispatch is handled by the `URLResolver` class (line 503); its `resolve(path)` method walks the URLconf patterns and returns a `ResolverMatch` (class at line 34) that maps the incoming path to a view callable + args/kwargs. (`URLResolver.resolve` is the method that resolves an incoming URL path to a view.)

tasks.task: Where does the ORM compile a QuerySet into a SQL SELECT statement?
tasks.without_answer: django/db/models/sql/compiler.py SQLCompiler.as_sql (line 757; class SQLCompiler at line 43; builds SELECT at line 813)
tasks.withdb_answer: django/db/models/sql/compiler.py â€” class SQLCompiler, method as_sql() (invoked via Query.get_compiler(...).as_sql()/execute_sql()). This is the ORM's core SELECT compiler. Note: this checkout's Axil index did not surface the base file with a line number; it consistently returned the database-backend subclasses that re-export/subclass this base SQLCompiler â€” django/db/backends/mysql/compiler.py (SQLUpdateCompiler:44, SQLDeleteCompiler:17) and django/db/backends/postgresql/compiler.py (SQLInsertCompiler:28), whose file proxies list "exports: SQLAggregateCompiler, SQLCompiler, SQLDeleteCompiler, SQLInsertCompiler, SQLUpdateCompiler" from django.db.models.sql.compiler.

tasks.task: Where does the ORM actually execute a query and materialize model instances?
tasks.withdb_answer: NOT LOCATABLE VIA THIS INDEX â€” the core ORM package `django/db/models/` is not indexed. Ground-truth (from Django architecture, unverifiable via Axil here): `django/db/models/query.py` â€” `ModelIterable.__iter__` executes the query (calls `SQLCompiler.execute_sql`) and materializes rows into model instances via `Model.from_db`; the raw SQL is run in `django/db/models/sql/compiler.py` â€” `SQLCompiler.execute_sql`, and `Model.from_db` lives in `django/db/models/base.py`. The index only contains backend SQLCompiler subclasses at `django/db/backends/mysql/compiler.py:1` and `django/db/backends/postgresql/compiler.py:1` (both `exports: SQLCompiler`), which override but do not perform base materialization.

tasks.task: Where does the template engine resolve a variable against the context during rendering?
tasks.without_answer: django/template/base.py Variable.resolve line 941 delegates to Variable._resolve_lookup line 965 which resolves a variable against the context.
tasks.withdb_answer: django/template/base.py â€” the `Variable` class (starts at line 867); its `Variable.resolve(self, context)` method resolves a variable against the template context during rendering (delegating to `Variable._resolve_lookup(context)` for attribute/dict/index lookups).

tasks.task: Where does Django build and apply the middleware chain around a view?
tasks.withdb_answer: django/core/handlers/base.py â€” class `BaseHandler` (line 21). `BaseHandler.load_middleware()` builds the chain (wrapping each middleware around `_get_response` into `self._middleware_chain`) and `BaseHandler.get_response()` applies that chain around the view.

tasks.without_answer: django/shortcuts.py â€” function `get_object_or_404`, defined at line 79.

tasks.task: Where does the migration autodetector compute the changes between two project states?
tasks.without_answer: django/db/migrations/autodetector.py â€” class MigrationAutodetector; public entry point changes() at line 61 delegates to the core computation _detect_changes() at line 126 (class defined at line 42, compares self.from_state vs self.to_state set in __init__ at line 55).
tasks.withdb_answer: django/db/migrations/autodetector.py â€” MigrationAutodetector class (defined at line 42). It "takes a pair of ProjectStates and compares them to see what the first would need doing to make it match the second." Public entry point: MigrationAutodetector.changes(); the actual change computation is done in the internal method MigrationAutodetector._detect_changes().

tasks.task: Where is the `Model` base class defined?
tasks.without_answer: django/db/models/base.py â€” `class Model(AltersData, metaclass=ModelBase)` at line 501
tasks.withdb_answer: django/db/models/base.py â€” class `Model` (the base class all Django models subclass as `models.Model`). NOTE: this checkout's Axil index does NOT contain a proxy for the core `django/db/models/base.py` file, so the exact defining line was NOT returned by Axil (`code-search "ModelBase"` â†’ "no code proxies matched"; `class Model`/`QuerySet` searches never surface that module). The file+symbol is inferred from the many indexed subclasses Axil DID return that reference it by name, e.g. `class Redirect(models.Model)` at django/contrib/redirects/models.py:6 and `class Group(models.Model)` at django/contrib/auth/models.py:107 â€” confirming the base class is `Model` in the `django.db.models` package.

tasks.task: Where does Django hash a raw password into its stored form?
tasks.without_answer: django/contrib/auth/hashers.py â€” make_password() (line 100) is the public entry point that turns a raw password into its stored form; it generates a salt and delegates to the hasher's encode() at line 118. The actual hashing for the default hasher is PBKDF2PasswordHasher.encode() (line 329): it calls pbkdf2() at line 333, base64-encodes the digest (line 334), and returns the stored "algorithm$iterations$salt$hash" string (line 335).
tasks.withdb_answer: django/contrib/auth/hashers.py â€” make_password() at line 100 (turns a raw password into its stored "algorithm$...$hash" form via the configured hasher's encode())

tasks.task: Where does the development server's autoreloader watch for file changes and restart?

ground_truth.notes: StatReloader (~401) or run_with_reloader (~681) â€” accept either.

tasks.without_answer: django/utils/autoreload.py â€” the development server autoreloader. The default reloader `StatReloader.tick` (line 404) polls file mtimes once per second (`SLEEP_TIME = 1`, line 402), pulling the watch list from `BaseReloader.watched_files` (line 315: all imported Python module files via `iter_all_python_module_files`, plus `extra_files` and directory globs). When a file's mtime increases it calls `BaseReloader.notify_file_changed` (line 386), which sends the `file_changed` signal and, if no receiver handles it, calls `trigger_reload` (line 268) â†’ `sys.exit(3)`. The parent process loop `restart_with_reloader` (line 273) re-spawns the child whenever it exits with return code 3, effecting the restart. (An optional `WatchmanReloader` at line 448 uses the Watchman service instead of mtime polling; `get_reloader` at line 658 picks between them, wired up by `run_with_reloader` at line 681.)
tasks.withdb_answer: django/utils/autoreload.py â€” the dev-server autoreloader. Watching + restart machinery: run_with_reloader (line 681) starts the loop; BaseReloader (line 295) tracks watched files/dirs and runs the change-detection tick; StatReloader (line 401, mtime polling) / WatchmanReloader (line 448) do the actual watching; restart_with_reloader (line 273) re-executes the process on change.

django/urls/base.py â€” `reverse()` function at line 28 (the public URL-reversing entry point). The underlying resolution is handled by `URLResolver` (with its `_reverse_with_prefix`) in django/urls/resolvers.py:503.

tasks.task: Where does a form field run its validation pipeline (to_python, validate, run_validators)?
tasks.without_answer: django/forms/fields.py â€” Field.clean() at line 203 runs the validation pipeline: it calls self.to_python(value) (line 208), self.validate(value) (line 209), and self.run_validators(value) (line 210), then returns the cleaned value.
tasks.withdb_answer: django/forms/fields.py â€” Field.clean() (base class `Field` starts at line 84). The `clean` method runs the validation pipeline: it calls self.to_python(value), then self.validate(value), then self.run_validators(value), and returns the cleaned value. The proxy signature confirms class Field exposes methods: to_python, validate, run_validators, clean.
